name: Abu Bakr as-Siddiq
epithet: The First to Believe Without Hesitation
title: Abu Bakr as-Siddiq (RA): The First to Believe Without Hesitation
meta_description: The story of Abu Bakr as-Siddiq (RA), the closest companion of the Prophet Muhammad and the first man to accept Islam. His honest trade, the slaves he freed, and the night in the cave.
keywords: Abu Bakr as-Siddiq, story of Abu Bakr, first man to accept Islam, companion in the cave, Bilal freed, companions of the Prophet, sahaba stories
lede: Some people hesitate when they hear something new. They weigh it, they wait, they ask what it will cost them. Abu Bakr was not one of these people. When the truth was put in front of him, he reached for it at once.

This is the story of Abu Bakr as-Siddiq (may Allah be pleased with him): the first man to accept Islam, the friend who emptied his wealth to free the weak, and the companion who sheltered with the Prophet Muhammad ﷺ in a cave while the whole of Makkah hunted them.

sections.h2: A noble name from a small tribe
paras: Abu Bakr was born around the year 572, about two and a half years after the Prophet Muhammad ﷺ. He came from Banu Taym, a small sub-tribe of Quraysh. It was not one of the great houses of Makkah, but it carried a good name. Banu Taym were known as people who kept out of the tribal wars and often played the role of peacemakers.

His name was Abdullah, the son of Abu Quhafa. He carried several nicknames, and each one tells you something. He loved raising camels, so people called him Abu Bakr, the father of the young camel. His mother, Umm al-Khayr, had lost sons before him, so when he survived she called him Atiq, the one who was freed: freed from death and allowed to live.

Years later the Prophet ﷺ gave that name a second meaning. He said that whoever wished to see a man freed from the Fire should look at Abu Bakr. The name his mother chose at his birth quietly pointed all the way to the end.

sections.h2: A heart that turned from the idols
paras: He grew into a sharp and gentle man. He loved poetry, but turned away from any verse that praised the idols. He studied the lineages of the Arabs and could trace any person back through their tribes. He was an honest merchant who dealt in cloth, and his honesty made people trust him, and that trust made him wealthy.

Even as a young man, Abu Bakr found idol worship empty. The story is told that his father once sat him before the idols of the Kaaba and told him to worship, then left. Abu Bakr spoke to one of them: clothe me, he said, then feed me. Nothing came. He picked up a stone and said, if you are a god, protect yourself. He threw it, and the idol toppled over.

He walked away from idol worship entirely and never returned to it. There is an echo of Ibrahim in this scene, the young man testing the idols and finding them powerless. And like Ibrahim, Abu Bakr would carry the title as-Siddiq, the truthful one, whose nature leaned towards the truth before he was ever asked to choose it.

sections.h2: The man who did not hesitate
paras: Abu Bakr and the Prophet ﷺ had been close friends since youth, for nearly forty years. They had travelled the trade routes together, and Abu Bakr had spent long, quiet hours in his company. He had seen the character up close. So when the Prophet ﷺ told him what had happened in the cave of Hira, that Allah had made him a Messenger, Abu Bakr did not pause. He said simply: I believe you.

The Prophet ﷺ later said that every person he invited to Islam had some hesitation at first, except Abu Bakr. He accepted at once and never wavered. By the reckoning of the scholars, he was the first man to enter Islam, as Khadijah was the first woman and Ali the first among the youth.

What is striking is what he did not stop to weigh: his rank, his wealth, what supporting this message would cost him. He found the truth, accepted it, and knew there would be a price. He paid it without complaint.

sections.h2: The doer of good, and all the good after it
paras: Abu Bakr did not stop at his own belief. He began calling others, and people listened, because he was respected and his word carried weight. Through his honest tongue, several of the early community came to faith, among them Uthman ibn Affan, Abdur-Rahman ibn Awf, az-Zubayr, Talhah, and Sa'd, names that would become pillars of Islam.

The Prophet ﷺ taught that the one who guides another to good receives the same reward as the doer, without anything taken from the doer. So every charity those companions later gave, and the good of all who came to faith through them, was written for Abu Bakr too, without lessening theirs in any way.

It is worth pausing on what that means. He did not only spend his own wealth and pray his own prayers. He opened doors, and the good that walked through them kept multiplying long after the moment had passed. A single sincere invitation can keep giving for the rest of your life.

sections.h2: Emptying his wealth to free the weak
paras: Islam first reached the weak and the enslaved, the people Makkah felt free to brutalise. Abu Bakr made it his work to find them and buy their freedom. The most famous was Bilal (may Allah be pleased with him), tortured under the desert sun, barely alive. His owner sneered that he was not worth a single coin, yet Abu Bakr paid in full and set him free. Umar would later call Bilal our master, freed by our master.

He freed others too, men and women, whether or not they could ever be of any use to him. He bought the freedom of a woman who had been beaten until she lost her sight, and of a slave girl who had been beaten by Umar himself, in the years before Umar accepted Islam. When his own father asked why he freed the weak ones who could do nothing for him, Abu Bakr answered that he sought only what was with Allah.

He had entered Islam with forty thousand dinars. By the time he migrated, only five thousand were left, most of it spent freeing the enslaved. The scholars say a passage of the Qur'an was revealed about exactly this:

ayah.text: The most pious one will be spared this, who gives his wealth away as self-purification, not to return a favour to anyone but for the sake of his Lord the Most High, and he will be well pleased.

sections.h2: Beaten for saying "my Lord is Allah"
paras: As the message spread, the persecution reached the Prophet ﷺ himself. One day, near the Kaaba, a man wrapped a cloth around his neck and began to choke him while he prayed. Abu Bakr heard, and could not hold himself back. He pushed through the crowd and cried out: would you kill a man for saying his Lord is Allah?

That was enough to turn the mob on him. They rubbed his face in the dirt and beat him until he fell unconscious, his face so swollen it could not be recognised. His tribesmen, not yet Muslims, carried him home, fearing he had died.

When he woke, he did not ask what had happened to him. His first words were: where is the Messenger of Allah? He would not rest until they carried him to the Prophet ﷺ so he could see with his own eyes that his friend was safe. His whole concern, even half-conscious and bloodied, was not himself.

When permission came to migrate to Madinah, the Prophet ﷺ came to Abu Bakr's house at an unusual hour. He told him he had been given leave to go, and that Abu Bakr was to be his companion on the road. The Prophet ﷺ was then the most hunted man in Makkah, with a bounty on his head, and he had just told Abu Bakr he would be at his side through the danger. Abu Bakr wept with joy. His daughter Aisha (may Allah be pleased with her) said she had never before known that a person could cry from happiness.

On the journey, Abu Bakr kept circling the Prophet ﷺ as they walked, moving ahead, then behind, then to each side. When asked why, he said he was thinking of every direction danger might come from, so that if anyone struck, it would strike him first. They hid in the tiny cave of Thawr, and while the Prophet ﷺ slept, Abu Bakr blocked a hole with his foot and let a scorpion sting him rather than wake his friend. His tears fell from the pain, but he did not move.

When their pursuers reached the very mouth of the cave, Abu Bakr trembled, not for himself, but for the Prophet ﷺ beside him. The Prophet ﷺ calmed him with the words the Qur'an would record forever: do not grieve, Allah is with us. And it was upon Abu Bakr, the companion in the cave, that Allah sent down His peace.

There is one more small picture from that road. Days into the journey, exhausted and without food or water, the two stopped at the tent of a woman who did not know who they were. The Prophet ﷺ was offered milk first, and he passed it to Abu Bakr. Abu Bakr refused, insisting the Prophet ﷺ drink first, three times over. Even his thirst waited on his friend's.

ayah.text: Even if you do not help the Prophet, God helped him when the disbelievers drove him out: when the two of them were in the cave, he [Muhammad] said to his companion, 'Do not worry, God is with us,' and God sent His calm down to him, aided him with forces invisible to you, and brought down the disbelievers' plan. God's plan is higher: God is almighty and wise.

lessons_heading: What Abu Bakr's life teaches us
lessons_intro: His story is not built on a single famous moment. It is built on a habit of responding to the truth at once, and of putting another before himself, again and again.

lessons.text: He did not weigh what belief would cost him before accepting it. When the truth was clear, he reached for it without hesitation.
He spent most of his fortune freeing people who could never repay him, seeking only what was with Allah.

lessons.bold: Real loyalty puts the other first.
lessons.text: Beaten unconscious, his first thought was for the Prophet ﷺ. In the cave, he feared only for his friend, not himself.
He shielded the Prophet ﷺ with his own body and his own name, and never sought any credit for it.

faq.a: He was the closest companion of the Prophet Muhammad ﷺ and the first man to accept Islam. A respected and honest merchant of Makkah from the tribe of Banu Taym, he is remembered for his unhesitating faith, his generosity, and his loyalty.

faq.q: Why is Abu Bakr called as-Siddiq?
faq.a: As-Siddiq means the truthful one, or the one who affirms the truth. His nature inclined towards truth before Islam, and he believed the Prophet ﷺ at once, without doubt, when others hesitated.

faq.q: How did Abu Bakr free the slaves?
faq.a: He used his own wealth to buy the freedom of enslaved people who were being tortured for their faith, the most famous being Bilal (RA). He freed men and women alike, whether or not they could be of any use to him, seeking only the reward of Allah.

faq.q: What happened in the cave of Thawr?
faq.a: During the migration to Madinah, the Prophet ﷺ and Abu Bakr hid in the cave of Thawr while Quraysh hunted them. When the pursuers reached the cave's mouth, the Prophet ﷺ reassured him: do not grieve, Allah is with us. This moment is recorded in the Qur'an.

closing_heading: Why this story stays with us
closing: It is easy to imagine that faith waits for proof, that we will commit once we are certain, once it is safe, once everyone else has moved first. Abu Bakr's life answers that quietly. He had spent years being honest in small things, and when the truth finally arrived he did not need to be argued into it. He simply said yes.

Most of our days are not made of grand decisions. They are made of small ones: a kindness we could give and ask nothing for, a moment we could put someone else ahead of ourselves, a right thing we could do before it becomes easy or admired. Be the one who does not pause when the good is clear. Carry that into today, and his story has done its work.
source_note: The narrative follows the account of Abu Bakr as-Siddiq (RA) in Dr. Omar Suleiman's series The Firsts (Yaqeen Institute), Part 1. Qur'an translations are from The Qur'an, M.A.S. Abdel Haleem (Qur'an 92:17-21 and 9:40). Where the historical record carries more than one narration, we have followed the most widely reported.

slides:
Some people hesitate when they hear something new.

They weigh it. They wait. They ask what it will cost them.

Abu Bakr was not one of these people.

He came from a small tribe with a good name.

An honest merchant, sharp and gentle, who grew rich on his honesty.

Even as a young man, he found the idols empty.

When the Prophet ﷺ told him he was now a Messenger of Allah, Abu Bakr did not pause.

He simply said: **I believe you.**

The first man to enter Islam.

Islam first reached the weak and the enslaved.

Abu Bakr made it his work to find them and buy their freedom.

The most famous was Bilal, tortured under the desert sun.

He entered Islam with forty thousand dinars.

He left Makkah with five thousand.

Most of it spent freeing people who could never repay him.

When the Prophet ﷺ was being choked near the Kaaba, Abu Bakr pushed through the mob.

"Would you kill a man for saying his Lord is Allah?"

They beat him unconscious.

He woke with his face too swollen to recognise.

His first words were not about himself.

They were: **where is the Messenger of Allah?**

**Say yes to the good without pausing.**

In the cave, he trembled not for himself, but for his friend.

"Do not grieve. Allah is with us."

May Allah be pleased with him.
social.caption:
The first man to accept Islam reached for the truth without a moment of doubt. Abu Bakr as-Siddiq (may Allah be pleased with him) was an honest merchant of Makkah, respected and wealthy, and a close friend of the Prophet ﷺ for nearly forty years.

When the message came, he believed at once. Then he spent most of his fortune buying the freedom of enslaved people who were being tortured for their faith, asking nothing in return. Beaten unconscious for defending the Prophet ﷺ, his first words on waking were about the safety of his friend, not himself.

Which part of his story stayed with you? 🤍

Save this for later, and share it with someone who would love it.

Read the full story: buruja.com/companions/abu-bakr-part-1

email.subject: The first man to believe said yes without hesitating
email.alt_subject: "Would you kill a man for saying his Lord is Allah?"
email.preview: He emptied his wealth to free the weak, and feared for the Prophet before himself.
email.body:
Assalamu alaikum,

Some people hesitate when they hear something new. They weigh it, they wait, they ask what it will cost them. Abu Bakr as-Siddiq (may Allah be pleased with him) was not one of these people.

He was an honest merchant of Makkah, respected and wealthy, and a close friend of the Prophet Muhammad ﷺ for nearly forty years. So when the Prophet ﷺ told him that Allah had made him a Messenger, Abu Bakr did not pause. He simply said: I believe you. The Prophet ﷺ later said that everyone he invited to Islam hesitated at first, except Abu Bakr. He was the first man to believe.

He did not stop at his own faith. Islam first reached the weak and the enslaved, the people Makkah felt free to brutalise, and Abu Bakr made it his work to find them and buy their freedom. The most famous was Bilal, tortured under the desert sun. He freed men and women alike, whether or not they could ever be of any use to him. When his own father asked why, he answered that he sought only what was with Allah. He had entered Islam with forty thousand dinars, and left Makkah with five thousand, most of it spent this way.

His loyalty cost him. When the Prophet ﷺ was being choked near the Kaaba, Abu Bakr pushed through the crowd and cried out, would you kill a man for saying his Lord is Allah? They beat him unconscious. When he woke, his first words were not about himself. They were: where is the Messenger of Allah?

There is a lesson in him that is easy to miss: real loyalty puts the other first. In the cave of Thawr, with their pursuers at the very mouth, he trembled not for his own life but for his friend's. And the Prophet ﷺ calmed him with words the Qur'an still carries: do not grieve, Allah is with us.

May Allah be pleased with him, and gather us in the company of those who were first.

If you would like the full story, we have written it here:

paras: Some people hesitate when they hear something new. They weigh it, they wait, they ask what it will cost them. Abu Bakr as-Siddiq (may Allah be pleased with him) was not one of these people. When the truth was put in front of him, he reached for it at once.

He was born about two and a half years after the Prophet Muhammad ﷺ, into a small tribe of Quraysh with a good name. He grew into a sharp and gentle man, an honest merchant who dealt in cloth, and his honesty was what made people trust him. Even as a young man he found the idols empty, and once toppled one with a thrown stone to show it could not protect itself.

He and the Prophet ﷺ had been friends since youth, for nearly forty years. So when the Prophet ﷺ told him what had happened, that Allah had made him a Messenger, Abu Bakr did not pause. He said simply, I believe you. He was the first man to enter Islam, and he never wavered. Then he began calling others, and because he was respected, people listened.

Islam first reached the weak and the enslaved, the people Makkah felt free to brutalise. Abu Bakr made it his work to find them and buy their freedom. The most famous was Bilal, tortured under the desert sun. He freed men and women alike, whether or not they could ever repay him. He had entered Islam with forty thousand dinars, and left Makkah with only five thousand, most of it spent this way, seeking only what was with Allah.

His loyalty was tested in the open. When the Prophet ﷺ was being choked near the Kaaba, Abu Bakr pushed through the mob, crying out, would you kill a man for saying his Lord is Allah? They beat him unconscious, his face too swollen to recognise. When he woke, his first words were not about himself. They were, where is the Messenger of Allah?

When the time came to migrate, the Prophet ﷺ chose him as his companion on the road, and Abu Bakr wept with joy. They hid together in the tiny cave of Thawr, and while the Prophet ﷺ slept, he let a scorpion sting his foot rather than wake him. When their pursuers reached the mouth of the cave, Abu Bakr trembled, not for himself, but for his friend. Do not grieve, the Prophet ﷺ told him. Allah is with us.
storybook.pullquote: Would you kill a man for saying his Lord is Allah?
storybook.reflection: Abu Bakr's faith was a quick yes, repeated for a lifetime. He believed without weighing the cost, gave without expecting return, and feared for his friend before himself. Most of our days are made of small choices like that: a kindness given for nothing, a moment we put someone else first, a right thing done before it becomes easy. To say yes without pausing is a quiet kind of greatness.
storybook.source: His story follows the account in Dr. Omar Suleiman's series The Firsts, Part 1.
